title: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection
exclusions: Does NOT detect pinning misconfiguration in client apps; see UC-22.38.3 for localization.
evidence: Hourly saved search 'tls_posture_audit' writes TLS scan findings to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 4.2 requires strong cryptography and security protocols; the UC flags weak ciphers and outdated protocols.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 4.2 (Strong cryptography for CHD in transit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.41.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: §164.312(e)(1) transmission security; TLS posture is the primary evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.312(e)(1) (Transmission security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.41.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(h) cryptography — the UC continuously validates posture.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SC-8(1) transmission confidentiality and integrity — implemented via strong TLS.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission confidentiality and integrity) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a TLS scan reveals a certificate expiring within 21 days, a protocol < TLS 1.2, or a non-AEAD cipher, the UC fires listing host, cert_expires_in, protocol, and cipher.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Hosts with TLS 1.2/1.3, AEAD ciphers, and certs ≥21 days from expiry do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=tls sourcetype=tls:scan earliest=-1h
| eval days_to_expiry=round((expires-now())/86400,0)
| where days_to_expiry<21 OR protocol!="TLSv1.2" AND protocol!="TLSv1.3" OR NOT match(cipher, "^TLS_(AES|CHACHA20|ECDHE)")
| table host days_to_expiry protocol cipher
description: Continuous TLS-posture audit replacing periodic scan spreadsheets. Any finding feeds remediation and the cipher-and-cert evidence pack.
value: Moves TLS posture from 'we scan quarterly' to 'we proved every TLS endpoint was compliant for every day of the period'.
implementation: (1) Run scanner (SSL Labs CLI, OpenSSL-based scripts, Qualys SSL Labs); (2) feed to Splunk; (3) schedule hourly; (4) on finding, ticket via SOAR. Indexes required: `index=tls`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=tls:scan`.
visualization: Table of non-compliant hosts, stacked bar of protocol distribution, single-value '% of endpoints with strong TLS'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `tls` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `4.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 4.2 (Strong cryptography for CHD in transit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `tls` under `tls:scan`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Run scanner (SSL Labs CLI, OpenSSL-based scripts, Qualys SSL Labs). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** feed to Splunk. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** schedule hourly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on finding, ticket via SOAR. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `days_to_expiry < 21`. For "Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert detection," the aggregation identifies records at risk of crossing a retention boundary — the control is about time-bound lifecycle enforcement, and the aggregate captures the current state of that lifecycle. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `21` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by days_to_expiry
| stats avg(days_to_expiry) as mean, perc50(days_to_expiry) as p50, perc90(days_to_expiry) as p90, perc99(days_to_expiry) as p99, max(days_to_expiry) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `21` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `count()` aggregation over the last hour is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.41.2: Certificate / TLS posture — weak cipher and expired-cert det]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -1h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.41.2,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=tls sourcetype=tls:scan earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `cipher`, `days_to_expiry`, `host`, `protocol`. Run:

```spl
index=tls sourcetype=tls:scan earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table cipher, days_to_expiry, host, protocol
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_to_expiry`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_to_expiry) as p50 perc90(days_to_expiry) as p90 perc99(days_to_expiry) as p99 max(days_to_expiry) as hi
```

The current threshold `21` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.41.2: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Non-Compliant Hosts (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Stacked Bar Of Protocol Distribution (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Endpoints With Strong Tls' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see encryption coverage and gaps so you can show protections stay current across systems.
